course_info.title: Research Methods in Psychology
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

class_schedule.topic: Science and Psychology: The Empirical Approach
learning_objectives: Explain why psychology is classified as a science
Distinguish between scientific and pseudoscientific claims
Describe the steps of the scientific method as applied to psychology
readings: Cozby & Bates, Methods in Behavioral Research, Ch. 1
Stanovich, How to Think Straight About Psychology, Ch. 1-2

lecture_notes.introduction: Psychology's status as a science depends on its commitment to systematic, empirical investigation. This course equips you with the tools to design, conduct, and evaluate psychological research.
key_concepts: Falsifiability as a criterion for scientific claims
Operationalization of psychological constructs
Theory-driven vs. data-driven research
The research cycle: observation, hypothesis, test, revision
Common threats to scientific thinking: confirmation bias, illusory correlation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluate three popular psychology claims using falsifiability and evidence criteria
setup_requirements: Access to PsycINFO or Google Scholar

review.value: Defining abstract constructs (e.g., anxiety) in terms of measurable indicators (e.g., score on the GAD-7)
A scientific hypothesis must be stated in a way that can potentially be proven wrong by evidence

learning_objectives: Summarize the APA ethical principles for research with human participants
Evaluate the ethical dimensions of classic studies (Milgram, Zimbardo, Tuskegee)
Describe the role and function of an Institutional Review Board
readings: APA Ethical Principles of Psychologists and Code of Conduct (selections)

lecture_notes.introduction: Ethical conduct is non-negotiable in research. Historical abuses led to the rigorous protections we have today.
key_concepts: Belmont Report principles: respect for persons, beneficence, justice
Informed consent, deception, and debriefing protocols
Risk-benefit analysis in research design
IRB review: exempt, expedited, and full board categories
Ethical treatment of animal subjects: IACUC oversight
lecture_notes.demonstration: Case study analysis: evaluate three research proposals for ethical concerns and propose modifications

review.value: Researchers must maximize benefits and minimize harm to participants
Permitted only when no alternatives exist, the study has significant value, and full debriefing follows

learning_objectives: Distinguish among nominal, ordinal, interval, and ratio scales of measurement
Define reliability and describe methods for assessing it
Explain construct validity, internal validity, and external validity

lecture_notes.introduction: Good research depends on good measurement. If our measures are unreliable or invalid, our conclusions are meaningless.
key_concepts: Four scales of measurement and their implications for analysis
Test-retest reliability, inter-rater reliability, internal consistency (Cronbach's alpha)
Face, content, criterion, and construct validity
Convergent and discriminant validity evidence
Measurement error and its impact on statistical power
lecture_notes.demonstration: Reliability exercise: students rate short essays using a rubric, then compute inter-rater reliability

review.value: A measure of internal consistency; values above 0.70 are generally considered acceptable
The degree to which a measure actually assesses the theoretical construct it claims to measure

learning_objectives: Design a between-subjects experiment with proper controls
Design a within-subjects experiment and address order effects
Identify and control for common confounding variables

lecture_notes.introduction: Experimental design is where the scientific method meets practical decision-making. Every design choice involves tradeoffs between internal validity, external validity, and feasibility.
key_concepts: Independent groups (between-subjects) design
Repeated measures (within-subjects) design
Counterbalancing: complete, Latin square, and randomized
Matched-pairs design
Confound identification and control strategies
lecture_notes.demonstration: Students redesign a flawed experiment, identifying three confounds and proposing solutions

review.value: Different participants in each condition; eliminates order effects but requires more participants
Systematically varying the order of conditions to control for practice and fatigue effects

learning_objectives: Evaluate the strengths and limitations of observational methods

lecture_notes.introduction: Not every question can be answered with a true experiment. Quasi-experimental and non-experimental designs are essential tools when random assignment is impossible or unethical.
key_concepts: Nonequivalent control group designs
Interrupted time series designs
Correlational studies: direction of causality problem
Naturalistic and systematic observation
Survey research: sampling methods and response biases
lecture_notes.demonstration: Evaluate a published quasi-experimental study, identifying threats to internal validity

review.value: Resembles an experiment but lacks random assignment; common when studying pre-existing groups
An unmeasured variable may account for an observed correlation between two other variables

learning_objectives: Structure a research report following APA format (Title, Abstract, Introduction, Method, Results, Discussion)
Write a clear and focused literature review
Present statistical results correctly in text and tables

lecture_notes.introduction: Scientific communication is as important as the research itself. APA format provides a standardized structure that allows researchers worldwide to efficiently share findings.
key_concepts: APA manuscript sections and their purpose
Writing an effective introduction with a funnel structure
Method section: participants, materials, procedure
Reporting statistics: notation, effect sizes, confidence intervals
Discussion: interpreting results, limitations, future directions
lecture_notes.demonstration: Annotated example of a published APA article, section by section

review.value: Report exact test statistics, degrees of freedom, p-values, and effect sizes: e.g., t(48) = 2.31, p = .025, d = 0.65
Introduction moves from broad topic to specific hypothesis, establishing context and rationale

questions.question: A researcher wants to study whether exposure to violent video games increases aggressive behavior. Describe how to operationalize both the independent variable and the dependent variable, and explain one potential confound that must be controlled.
questions.answer: The IV (violent video games) could be operationalized as 30 minutes of playing a game rated M for violence vs. 30 minutes of a non-violent game matched for difficulty and arousal. The DV (aggressive behavior) could be operationalized using the Taylor Aggression Paradigm (setting noise blast intensity for a fictitious opponent). A potential confound is pre-existing aggression levels; this should be controlled via random assignment or by measuring baseline aggression.

rubric.key: Identifies a valid confound and control strategy

questions.question: Explain the difference between reliability and validity, and describe a scenario in which a measure could be reliable but not valid.
questions.answer: Reliability is the consistency of a measure; validity is the accuracy with which it measures what it claims to measure. A bathroom scale that consistently reads 5 pounds too heavy is reliable (consistent readings) but not valid (inaccurate). Similarly, a personality test that consistently gives the same scores on retest but actually measures social desirability rather than the intended trait is reliable but not valid.

rubric.key: Correct definitions of reliability and validity
Provides a clear example of reliable but not valid

questions.question: A school district wants to evaluate a new anti-bullying program. Random assignment of students to program vs. no-program conditions is not feasible. Propose a quasi-experimental design and identify two threats to internal validity.
questions.answer: A nonequivalent control group pretest-posttest design: implement the program in one school and use a demographically similar school as the comparison group. Measure bullying incidents before and after. Threats: (1) Selection bias -- the schools may differ in ways that affect outcomes; (2) History -- events at one school (e.g., a bullying incident that triggers changes) could confound the program's effect.

rubric.key: Identifies two legitimate threats to internal validity
Explains why each threat is relevant

questions.question: Explain why counterbalancing is necessary in a within-subjects design and describe the Latin square approach.
questions.answer: In within-subjects designs, the same participants experience all conditions, creating the risk of order effects (practice, fatigue, carryover). Counterbalancing controls this by varying the sequence of conditions across participants. A Latin square is an efficient counterbalancing method where each condition appears in each ordinal position exactly once across participant groups, requiring fewer orders than complete counterbalancing.

rubric.key: Explains order effects as the reason for counterbalancing

major_assignments.title: Research Proposal
major_assignments.description: Develop a complete research proposal for an original psychological study. Include a literature review, clearly stated hypotheses, a detailed method section, proposed analysis plan, and discussion of ethical considerations. The proposal must be in APA format.
objectives: Formulate a testable research hypothesis based on existing literature
Design an appropriate methodology including sampling, measurement, and procedure
Identify potential confounds and describe control strategies
Address ethical considerations and propose IRB-compliant procedures
deliverables: Literature review with minimum 10 peer-reviewed sources
Detailed method section (participants, materials, procedure, design)
Ethics section with completed IRB checklist

criteria: Thorough review of relevant prior research
Clear identification of gap in the literature
Logical development of hypothesis from existing findings
Proper APA citations
Appropriate research design for the hypothesis
Clear operationalization of variables
Adequate sampling plan with power considerations
Detailed, replicable procedure
Statistical tests match the design and hypotheses
Addresses assumptions of proposed analyses
Includes effect size and power considerations
Describes how results will be interpreted
Comprehensive ethical analysis
APA format compliance
Clear, professional writing
Complete and accurate reference list
Well-justified hypothesis grounded in existing literature
Rigorous methodology with appropriate controls
Correct and complete statistical analysis plan
Thorough ethical considerations and professional APA writing

weekly_quizzes.description: Regular assessment of methodological concepts and terminology

lab_assignments.description: Hands-on exercises in research design, data collection, and analysis

major_assignments.description: Original research proposal demonstrating mastery of research methods

midterm_examination.description: Comprehensive exam on research design and methodology

final_examination.description: Cumulative exam including design critique and proposal evaluation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused lab absences will result in a failing grade for the lab component.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments are penalized 10% per day. Research proposals submitted late will not receive written feedback.
course_policies.collaboration: Lab exercises may be completed in pairs, but all written assignments must be independently authored. Data sharing is permitted; writing is not.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Fabrication or falsification of data is treated as a serious ethical violation. All work must be original and properly cited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to SPSS or JASP statistical software, university library databases, and APA citation management tools.
